main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Recurrent diffusion for large-scale Parameter Generation (RPG): divides trained parameters into non-overlapping parts ('tokens'), uses a recurrent model to learn relationships among these parts, and feeds the outputs as conditions into a diffusion model to generate neural network parameters."],"problems":["Scaling up neural network parameter generation to large models under memory constraints.","Modeling and preserving correlations among parameter parts during generation.","Generating parameters for large-scale vision and language models using limited computational resources (e.g., a single GPU)."],"datasets":["Not explicitly specified in the provided content, but mentions use of datasets for training vision and language models (e.g., datasets for ResNet, ViT, ConvNeXt, and LLaMA-7B)."],"metrics":["Model performance (e.g., accuracy or task-specific metrics) compared to original trained networks."],"results":[{"metric":"Performance of generated parameters vs. original models","value":"Comparable results across various architectures and tasks."}],"novelty_claims":["First method to enable large-scale parameter generation (e.g., ConvNeXt-L, LoRA parameters of LLaMA-7B) using only a single GPU.","Proposes a recurrent model to explicitly model and leverage correlations among parameter parts, which previous methods overlooked.","Demonstrates potential for generating models for unseen tasks, suggesting improved generalization and practicality.","Introduces a new approach that treats parameter parts as tokens and models their relationships, inspired by techniques in vision and language modeling."]}

methods: Recurrent diffusion for large-scale Parameter Generation (RPG): divides trained parameters into non-overlapping parts ('tokens'), uses a recurrent model to learn relationships among these parts, and feeds the outputs as conditions into a diffusion model to generate neural network parameters.
problems: Scaling up neural network parameter generation to large models under memory constraints.
Modeling and preserving correlations among parameter parts during generation.
Generating parameters for large-scale vision and language models using limited computational resources (e.g., a single GPU).
datasets: Not explicitly specified in the provided content, but mentions use of datasets for training vision and language models (e.g., datasets for ResNet, ViT, ConvNeXt, and LLaMA-7B).
metrics: Model performance (e.g., accuracy or task-specific metrics) compared to original trained networks.

results.metric: Performance of generated parameters vs. original models
results.value: Comparable results across various architectures and tasks.

novelty_claims: First method to enable large-scale parameter generation (e.g., ConvNeXt-L, LoRA parameters of LLaMA-7B) using only a single GPU.
Proposes a recurrent model to explicitly model and leverage correlations among parameter parts, which previous methods overlooked.
Demonstrates potential for generating models for unseen tasks, suggesting improved generalization and practicality.
Introduces a new approach that treats parameter parts as tokens and models their relationships, inspired by techniques in vision and language modeling.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Neural Network Diffusion (p-diff): a method that uses an autoencoder to extract latent representations of neural network parameters and a standard latent diffusion model to generate new latent representations, which are then decoded to produce new high-performing network parameters"],"problems":["Generating high-performing neural network parameters using generative models","Exploring the use of diffusion models beyond image and video generation, specifically for neural network parameter generation","Avoiding memorization of training models when generating new parameters"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100"],"metrics":["Model performance (e.g., accuracy) compared to trained networks","Prediction diversity (difference in predictions between generated and trained models)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First to demonstrate that diffusion models can generate high-performing neural network parameters, not just images or videos","Proposes a simple approach combining an autoencoder and a diffusion model for parameter generation","Shows that generated models achieve comparable or improved performance over trained networks with minimal additional cost","Empirically demonstrates that generated models do not memorize the training models, but synthesize novel parameters"]}

methods: Neural Network Diffusion (p-diff): a method that uses an autoencoder to extract latent representations of neural network parameters and a standard latent diffusion model to generate new latent representations, which are then decoded to produce new high-performing network parameters
problems: Generating high-performing neural network parameters using generative models
Exploring the use of diffusion models beyond image and video generation, specifically for neural network parameter generation
Avoiding memorization of training models when generating new parameters
metrics: Model performance (e.g., accuracy) compared to trained networks
Prediction diversity (difference in predictions between generated and trained models)
novelty_claims: First to demonstrate that diffusion models can generate high-performing neural network parameters, not just images or videos
Proposes a simple approach combining an autoencoder and a diffusion model for parameter generation
Shows that generated models achieve comparable or improved performance over trained networks with minimal additional cost
Empirically demonstrates that generated models do not memorize the training models, but synthesize novel parameters

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): freezing pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture"],"problems":["Reducing the number of trainable parameters required for adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks","Reducing GPU memory requirements and hardware barriers for fine-tuning large language models","Avoiding increased inference latency and sequence length reduction introduced by other parameter-efficient adaptation methods","Maintaining or improving model quality compared to full fine-tuning while increasing efficiency"],"datasets":["RoBERTa","DeBERTa","GPT-2","GPT-3"],"metrics":["Model quality (e.g., accuracy, perplexity, or task-specific metrics, though not specified in the excerpt)","Number of trainable parameters","GPU memory requirement","Training throughput","Inference latency"],"results":[{"metric":"Reduction in trainable parameters (GPT-3 175B)","value":"10,000x fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning"},{"metric":"Reduction in GPU memory requirement","value":"3x less GPU memory required compared to full fine-tuning"},{"metric":"Model quality","value":"On-par or better than full fine-tuning on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LoRA, a method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers while freezing pre-trained weights, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters needed for adaptation.","LoRA achieves comparable or better model quality than full fine-tuning with significantly fewer trainable parameters and lower memory requirements.","LoRA introduces no additional inference latency, unlike other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.","LoRA is orthogonal to and can be combined with other adaptation methods such as prefix-tuning.","Provides empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of language model adaptation, supporting the efficacy of LoRA."]}

methods: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): freezing pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture
problems: Reducing the number of trainable parameters required for adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks
Reducing GPU memory requirements and hardware barriers for fine-tuning large language models
Avoiding increased inference latency and sequence length reduction introduced by other parameter-efficient adaptation methods
Maintaining or improving model quality compared to full fine-tuning while increasing efficiency
metrics: Model quality (e.g., accuracy, perplexity, or task-specific metrics, though not specified in the excerpt)

results.value: 10,000x fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning
3x less GPU memory required compared to full fine-tuning
On-par or better than full fine-tuning on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3

novelty_claims: Proposes LoRA, a method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers while freezing pre-trained weights, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters needed for adaptation.
LoRA achieves comparable or better model quality than full fine-tuning with significantly fewer trainable parameters and lower memory requirements.
LoRA introduces no additional inference latency, unlike other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.
LoRA is orthogonal to and can be combined with other adaptation methods such as prefix-tuning.
Provides empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of language model adaptation, supporting the efficacy of LoRA.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Tina: Text-Conditioned Neural Network Diffusion framework","Diffusion transformer (DiT) model conditioned on task descriptions","CLIP model for embedding user task descriptions as conditions for diffusion","Train-once-for-all personalization paradigm"],"problems":["Text-to-model generation: generating personalized model parameters from text prompts","Generalization to unseen tasks and entities with limited training data","Personalization of models for diverse end-users and tasks using only text prompts","Scalability to astronomical numbers of possible personalized tasks"],"datasets":["CIFAR-100"],"metrics":["In-distribution generalization accuracy","Out-of-distribution generalization accuracy","Zero-shot/few-shot generalization performance"],"results":[{"metric":"Number of personalized tasks supported","value":"1.73 × 10^13 (for 10-way tasks on CIFAR-100)"},{"metric":"Training dataset size required for generalization","value":"~1000 data points"},{"metric":"Generalization","value":"Demonstrates both in-distribution and out-of-distribution generalization on unseen tasks and entities"}],"novelty_claims":["First paper to use text prompts as conditions for neural network diffusion to generate model parameters (text-to-model generation)","Proposes Tina, a text-conditioned neural network diffusion framework for train-once-for-all personalization","Demonstrates that Tina can generalize to unseen tasks and entities even with small model datasets","Shows that vision-language alignment (via CLIP) enables few-shot and zero-shot generalization from images and natural language prompts","Analyzes the boundaries and abilities of text-conditioned neural network diffusion for model personalization"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Trajectory Stitching (T-Stitch): using a small DPM for early denoising steps and switching to a large DPM for later steps","Applying T-Stitch as a training-free, drop-in technique to accelerate pre-trained diffusion models","Optionally fine-tuning the large DPM only on the timesteps it is applied for further quality improvement"],"problems":["High computational cost and slow sampling in diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) for high-quality image generation","Inefficiency of using a single large DPM throughout the entire sampling trajectory","Degraded prompt alignment in stylized Stable Diffusion models"],"datasets":["ImageNet (class-conditional)"],"metrics":["FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)","Sampling time (seconds)","Prompt alignment (qualitative)"],"results":[{"metric":"FID (ImageNet, 100 DDIM steps, 40% DiT-S steps)","value":"No degradation compared to full DiT-XL"},{"metric":"Sampling time (8 images, RTX 3090, 100 steps, DiT-XL)","value":"16.5 seconds"},{"metric":"Sampling time (8 images, RTX 3090, 100 steps, DiT-S)","value":"1.7 seconds"},{"metric":"Sampling time (8 images, RTX 3090, 100 steps, T-Stitch with 40% DiT-S)","value":"~1.5x speedup over DiT-XL with no FID drop"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes T-Stitch, a simple, training-free method to accelerate sampling in pre-trained diffusion models by stitching together different model sizes along the sampling trajectory","Demonstrates that a small DPM can be used for early denoising steps without loss in generation quality, enabling significant speedup","Shows that T-Stitch is generally applicable to different architectures and samplers, and complements existing fast sampling techniques","Finds that T-Stitch can improve prompt alignment in stylized Stable Diffusion models, in addition to accelerating them","Establishes that T-Stitch achieves better quality-efficiency trade-offs than previous model stitching and training-based interpolation methods"]}

methods: Trajectory Stitching (T-Stitch): using a small DPM for early denoising steps and switching to a large DPM for later steps
Applying T-Stitch as a training-free, drop-in technique to accelerate pre-trained diffusion models
Optionally fine-tuning the large DPM only on the timesteps it is applied for further quality improvement
problems: High computational cost and slow sampling in diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) for high-quality image generation
Inefficiency of using a single large DPM throughout the entire sampling trajectory
Degraded prompt alignment in stylized Stable Diffusion models

results.value: No degradation compared to full DiT-XL

results.metric: Sampling time (8 images, RTX 3090, 100 steps, DiT-XL)
Sampling time (8 images, RTX 3090, 100 steps, DiT-S)
Sampling time (8 images, RTX 3090, 100 steps, T-Stitch with 40% DiT-S)
results.value: ~1.5x speedup over DiT-XL with no FID drop

novelty_claims: Proposes T-Stitch, a simple, training-free method to accelerate sampling in pre-trained diffusion models by stitching together different model sizes along the sampling trajectory
Demonstrates that a small DPM can be used for early denoising steps without loss in generation quality, enabling significant speedup
Shows that T-Stitch is generally applicable to different architectures and samplers, and complements existing fast sampling techniques
Finds that T-Stitch can improve prompt alignment in stylized Stable Diffusion models, in addition to accelerating them
Establishes that T-Stitch achieves better quality-efficiency trade-offs than previous model stitching and training-based interpolation methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Selective State Space Models (Selective SSMs)","Input-dependent parameterization of SSMs","Hardware-aware parallel recurrent algorithm for SSMs","Mamba architecture: simplified, homogenous neural network using selective SSMs without attention or MLP blocks"],"problems":["Inefficiency of Transformers for long sequence modeling due to quadratic scaling","Subquadratic-time architectures (linear attention, gated convolution, SSMs) underperforming on discrete, information-dense modalities like language","Inability of prior SSMs to perform content-based reasoning and selective information propagation","Need for a general, efficient, and high-quality sequence model backbone for foundation models across modalities"],"datasets":["Synthetic tasks (copying, induction heads)","Audio waveform datasets (including challenging speech generation datasets)","DNA sequence datasets (genomics)","Language modeling datasets (including those used for LLaMa and Pythia baselines)"],"metrics":["Pretraining perplexity (language modeling)","Downstream evaluation metrics (e.g., common sense reasoning accuracy)","Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for speech generation","Throughput (generation speed)","Scaling laws (performance vs. model size)"],"results":[{"metric":"Generation throughput","value":"5× higher than Transformers of similar size"},{"metric":"Pretraining perplexity (language modeling)","value":"Mamba matches or exceeds Transformers of same size; selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Dynamic Tuning (DyT): a method for ViT adaptation that improves both parameter and inference efficiency","Token dispatcher: dynamically determines which tokens are informative and allows less important tokens to skip transformer blocks","Lightweight adapter modules: used in conjunction with token dispatcher for efficient adaptation","Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-based adapter: enhances token processing with negligible additional FLOPs","Four model variants of DyT to explore best practices"],"problems":["Lack of inference efficiency in existing FLOPs compared to existing PEFT methods"},{"metric":"Accuracy (VTAB-1K)","value":"DyT surpasses existing PEFT methods"},{"metric":"FLOPs reduction (K400, SSV2)","value":"DyT reduces computation by 37GFLOPs"},{"metric":"FLOPs reduction (ADE20K)","value":"DyT reduces computation by 21GFLOPs"},{"metric":"Accuracy (ADE20K)","value":"DyT outperforms full tuning"}],"novelty_claims":["First approach during adaptation","Design of a MoE-based adapter for further boosting adaptation performance with negligible computational overhead","Demonstration that DyT is suitable for both visual recognition and dense prediction tasks, unlike prior token pruning methods","Comprehensive evaluation across image, video, and segmentation tasks showing superior efficiency and performance"]}

methods: Dynamic Tuning (DyT): a method for ViT adaptation that improves both parameter and inference efficiency
Token dispatcher: dynamically determines which tokens are informative and allows less important tokens to skip transformer blocks
Lightweight adapter modules: used in conjunction with token dispatcher for efficient adaptation
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-based adapter: enhances token processing with negligible additional FLOPs
Four model variants of DyT to explore best practices
problems: Lack of inference efficiency in existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods for ViT adaptation
High computational cost of adapting large pre-trained ViT models to downstream tasks
Limited applicability of existing dynamic networks and token pruning methods to domain transfer and dense prediction tasks
metrics: Generalization ability (implied by performance on multiple datasets)

results.value: DyT uses only 71% of the FLOPs compared to existing PEFT methods

novelty_claims: First approach to unify both parameter and inference efficiency for ViT adaptation in a single framework
Introduction of a token dispatcher that dynamically skips transformer blocks for less informative tokens during adaptation
Design of a MoE-based adapter for further boosting adaptation performance with negligible computational overhead
Demonstration that DyT is suitable for both visual recognition and dense prediction tasks, unlike prior token pruning methods
Comprehensive evaluation across image, video, and segmentation tasks showing superior efficiency and performance

of previous hyper-representation approaches.","SANE can generate unseen neural network models and sample targeted models by prompting with different architectures, which was unattainable with previous methods: Sequential Autoencoder for Neural Embeddings (SANE)
Layer-wise sequential processing of neural network weights for embedding
Task-agnostic hyper-representation learning using transformer-based autoencoders
problems: Scalability of weight-space learning to large neural networks
Task-specificity of previous weight-space learning methods (discriminative vs. generative)
Inability of previous hyper-representation methods to process large or varied architectures
Learning representations of neural network weights that generalize across tasks and architectures
datasets: Model zoos of CNNs and ResNets
metrics: Accuracy (for discriminative and generative downstream tasks)

results.metric: Accuracy improvement for initialization (generative task, small CNNs)
Accuracy improvement for finetuning to new tasks (generative task, small CNNs)
Accuracy improvement for initialization (generative task, large ResNets)
Accuracy improvement for finetuning to new tasks (generative task, large ResNets)

novelty_claims: SANE is the first scalable, task-agnostic weight-space learning method that can handle large neural networks of varying architectures.
SANE enables both discriminative and generative downstream tasks from a single learned representation, unlike previous methods that required task-specific models.
SANE introduces sequential, layer-wise embedding of neural network weights, overcoming the size limitations of previous hyper-representation approaches.
SANE can generate unseen neural network models and sample targeted models by prompting with different architectures, which was unattainable with previous methods.
SANE demonstrates state-of-the-art or superior performance on both small and large model zoos, including tasks and architectures previously out of reach for hyper-representation learning.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Graph conditional latent diffusion framework (GNN-Diff)","Task-oriented graph autoencoder for conditioning parameter generation"],"problems":["High computational cost and human effort required for hyperparameter tuning in GNNs","Overfitting and limited generalization due to grid search and search space design in GNN training","Need for an alternative to heavy tuning while preserving or improving model accuracy"],"datasets":["Not specified in the provided content"],"metrics":["Node classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Node classification accuracy","value":"GNN-Diff produces GNN parameters that outperform those obtained through comprehensive grid search"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a graph conditional latent diffusion framework (GNN-Diff) to generate high-performing GNN parameters directly from coarse search checkpoints, reducing the need for heavy hyperparameter tuning.","Integrates data and graph structural information as a condition for GNN parameter generation using a task-oriented graph autoencoder.","Demonstrates that GNN-Diff can outperform parameters obtained from comprehensive grid search and diffusion frameworks designed for general neural networks.","Provides an alternative to extensive tuning and complex search space design by sampling reliable parameters directly."]}

methods: Graph conditional latent diffusion framework (GNN-Diff)

problems: High computational cost and human effort required for hyperparameter tuning in GNNs

results.value: GNN-Diff produces GNN parameters that outperform those obtained through comprehensive grid search

novelty_claims: Proposes a graph conditional latent diffusion framework (GNN-Diff) to generate high-performing GNN parameters directly from coarse search checkpoints, reducing the need for heavy hyperparameter tuning.
Integrates data and graph structural information as a condition for GNN parameter generation using a task-oriented graph autoencoder.
Demonstrates that GNN-Diff can outperform parameters obtained from comprehensive grid search and diffusion frameworks designed for general neural networks.
Provides an alternative to extensive tuning and complex search space design by sampling reliable parameters directly.

methods: DeepCache: a training-free acceleration algorithm for diffusion models that caches and retrieves high-level features across adjacent denoising steps to reduce redundant computations

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Diffusion Transformers (DiTs): replacing the U-Net backbone in latent diffusion models with a transformer operating on latent patches","Scaling DiT models by increasing transformer depth, width, and number of input tokens","Training DiTs within the Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) framework"],"problems":["Limited adoption of transformer architectures in diffusion models for image generation","Assessing the scalability and performance of transformer-based backbones versus U-Net in diffusion models","Establishing empirical baselines for transformer-based diffusion models"],"datasets":["ImageNet 512×512","ImageNet 256×256"],"metrics":["FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)","Gflops (forward pass complexity)"],"results":[{"metric":"FID (ImageNet 256×256, class-conditional)","value":"2.27 (state-of-the-art)"},{"metric":"Correlation between Gflops and FID","value":"Higher Gflops (larger models) consistently yield lower FID"}],"novelty_claims":["First to systematically replace the U-Net backbone in diffusion models with a transformer operating on latent patches (Diffusion Transformers, DiTs)","Demonstrate that U-Net inductive bias is not crucial for diffusion model performance; transformers can be used instead","Show that DiTs are highly scalable and outperform all prior diffusion models on class-conditional ImageNet 256×256 and 512×512 benchmarks","Establish a strong correlation between model complexity (Gflops) and sample quality (FID) in transformer-based diffusion models"]}

results.value: Higher Gflops (larger models) consistently yield lower FID

novelty_claims: First to systematically replace the U-Net backbone in diffusion models with a transformer operating on latent patches (Diffusion Transformers, DiTs)
Demonstrate that U-Net inductive bias is not crucial for diffusion model performance; transformers can be used instead

Establish a strong correlation between model complexity (Gflops) and sample quality (FID) in transformer-based diffusion models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Weight-Decomposed Low-Rank Adaptation (DoRA)","Novel weight decomposition analysis (decomposing weights into magnitude and direction)","Employing LoRA for directional updates in fine-tuning"],"problems":["Accuracy gap between parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods (like LoRA) and full fine-tuning (FT)","Limited learning capacity and training stability of existing PEFT methods compared to FT","Need for parameter-efficient fine-tuning without additional inference cost"],"datasets":["LLaMA (LLaMA-7B, LLaMA-13B)","LLaMA2-7B","LLaMA3-8B","LLaVA-7B","VL-BART"],"metrics":["Accuracy (on commonsense reasoning tasks)","Performance on visual instruction tuning","Performance on image/video-text understanding"],"results":[{"metric":"Commonsense reasoning accuracy (LLaMA-7B/13B)","value":"+3.7 / +1.0 over LoRA"},{"metric":"Commonsense reasoning accuracy (LLaMA2-7B)","value":"+2.9 over LoRA"},{"metric":"Commonsense reasoning accuracy (LLaMA3-8B)","value":"+4.4 over LoRA"},{"metric":"Visual instruction tuning (LLaVA-7B)","value":"+0.6 over LoRA"},{"metric":"Image/video-text understanding (VL-BART)","value":"+0.9 / +1.9 over LoRA"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of DoRA, a PEFT method that decomposes pre-trained weights into magnitude and direction for fine-tuning, closely resembling the learning capacity of full fine-tuning.","Novel weight decomposition analysis to reveal fundamental differences in learning patterns between FT and PEFT methods.","DoRA achieves higher accuracy and training stability than LoRA without additional inference cost, across a variety of tasks and model backbones."]}

Propose a text-guided contrastive adversarial training loss (TeCoA) that aligns adversarial visual features with text embeddings to retain zero-shot generalization while improving robustness.
Demonstrate that visual prompt tuning is more effective than finetuning when text is unavailable, and that finetuning with TeCoA is superior when text is available.
Establish a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for zero-shot adversarial robustness.
